# The Weaver's Gift

Long ago, the night sky was empty and black, causing great fear among the people. They huddled in their homes when darkness fell, trembling at the void above.

In those times, there lived a humble weaver named Astara, whose fabrics shimmered with threads of pure light. One evening, the Moon Goddess appeared before her, weeping silver tears.

"Why do you cry?" asked Astara.

"My sister, the Night, is lonely and feared," the goddess replied. "No one sees her beauty—only emptiness."

Astara's heart filled with compassion. She worked through the night, weaving her most luminous threads into thousands of tiny points of light. As dawn approached, she climbed the highest mountain and cast her creation skyward.

The threads scattered across the heavens, each one catching and holding fast to the darkness. They began to glow, transforming the night into a tapestry of wonder.

The Moon Goddess smiled. "You have given my sister companions—the stars. For your kindness, your own light shall burn brightest among them."

And so Astara became the North Star, forever guiding travelers through the darkness she had made beautiful. Since that night, humans no longer fear the dark, for they know the weaver watches over them still.

# The Moon Weaver's Loom

Long ago, when the world was newly formed, the Moon was whole and brilliant every night. She was a skilled weaver who created the fabric of dreams for all sleeping creatures.

The Sun, jealous of the Moon's nightly admirers, challenged her: "If you are truly the greater artisan, weave me a cloak that captures the essence of night itself."

The Moon accepted. Each night, she tore pieces from herself, spinning her own silver light into thread. As she worked, she grew smaller and smaller—full, then half, then just a crescent sliver. The creatures below watched in wonder as she seemed to disappear.

But when the cloak was complete, it was so magnificent that even the Sun was moved. "I cannot accept such a sacrifice," he said, returning the cloak to her.

The Moon unraveled her work, weaving herself whole again thread by thread, growing from crescent to full once more.

Now, the Moon continues this eternal cycle—waxing and waning—not from the Sun's jealousy, but as a reminder: true artistry requires both giving of oneself and the wisdom to restore what is depleted. Every month, she demonstrates that even in our emptiest moments, we can become whole again.
